prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. She has dyslexia and finds writing really hard. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

## What Works Really Well

**Narrative structure & honesty**: This is a complete, engaging story with clear stakes and a genuine emotional arc. It doesn't feel like an assignment—it feels like something she actually cares about.

**Specific details**: The concrete examples (pick and roll, fast break drill, "stay out of the way") make this vivid. These details show rather than tell.

**Authentic voice**: The casual, conversational tone ("Obviously." "which sounds like a weird pivot") makes this feel real. That's hard to fake and it's a real strength.

**Meaningful reflection**: She identifies *layers* of learning—not just "I learned failure is okay" but specific insights about casual vs. competitive contexts, the Dunning-Kruger element ("you don't know what you don't know"), and how failure redirected her toward a better fit. That's sophisticated thinking.

**Meets assignment**: Clear personal narrative about failure + learning, right around 500 words, appropriate scope.

---

- "I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did" is great—could lean into showing that moment a bit more (what did she say? how did mom respond?)
- The transition from "really upset" to joining debate moves quickly; even one sentence bridging those could help

---

## Bottom Line

This is solid work, especially considering dyslexia adds real cognitive load to writing. The bones are excellent—structure, voice, honesty, reflection. The feedback above is about polish, not substance. She should feel good about this essay.

I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. She has dyslexia and finds writing really hard. Can you help? The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

## What Works Really Well

**Narrative structure & specific details** — This has a clear arc (confidence → humbling tryout experience → failure → pivot to debate) with vivid moments ("stay out of the way," the pick-and-roll confusion, Marcus making it). These concrete details make it compelling.

**Authentic voice** — This sounds like a real teenager reflecting, not someone performing "proper essay writing." That's a strength, not a weakness.

Instead she gets at something more interesting: the gap between casual competence and actual skill, and how that failure redirected her somewhere better. The final insight about "you don't know what you don't know" shows real thinking.

**Shows growth** — Moving to debate isn't just a recovery story; it's evidence that she learned something *actionable* from the failure.

## Room to Improve

1. **Show don't tell in one spot** — "I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did" could be more vivid (what did you actually do/say instead?). Just one or two details here would land harder.

2. **Strengthen the ending** — The final paragraph gets a bit abstract/preachy. End with something more concrete or memorable—maybe revisit a specific moment or image.

This is honest, well-structured, and shows real reflection. She understands the assignment and executes it well. Well done.
